content.text: An output metric measures what the AI system does: latency, token count, accuracy on a test set, sessions completed. An outcome metric measures what changes in the world as a result: time saved, revenue protected, error rate reduced, customer satisfaction improved. Only outcome metrics justify investment; output metrics are diagnostic tools that explain why outcomes are or aren't being met.

The confusion between the two is the most common failure mode in GenAI measurement. A team that tracks 'sessions per day' and 'tokens generated' can show a chart that goes up and to the right while the product is silently failing to deliver value — and will not discover the failure until a business review asks why costs did not fall.

attrs.alt: Outcome vs output metric hierarchy for a GenAI FNOL chatbot

content.text: Traditional software has a tighter coupling between system behaviour and business outcome: if the checkout page completes orders reliably, orders are placed. GenAI is probabilistic: a chatbot can complete a session, return a confident response, and still deliver wrong information that causes rework downstream. The signal that system behaviour correlates with business outcomes cannot be assumed — it must be verified.
GenAI outputs can be locally coherent and globally wrong (hallucination). Session completion tells you nothing about whether the output was correct.
GenAI quality degrades gradually after model updates, prompt drift, or data distribution shift. Output metrics without outcome linkage will not detect slow-burn quality decay.
GenAI costs scale with usage. High session counts without outcome linkage cannot distinguish productive from wasteful usage.
GenAI decisions can be biased by demographic signals in the input. Without outcome stratification by group, fairness failures are invisible.

Enterprise GenAI features require metrics at three levels, linked in a chain: strategic outcomes (annual, financial) depend on outcome metrics (monthly, operational), which are explained by output metrics (daily, technical).
Cost per claim reduced 18%; NPS +12 pts
No business case can be validated
Cannot explain whether AI is causing strategic improvement
Hallucination rate; escalation rate; TTFT P95
Cannot diagnose why outcomes are or aren't met

Each layer asks a different question. Strategic: did this product justify its investment? Outcome: is the product changing user and business behaviour as intended? Output: is the AI component performing within acceptable parameters?

fnol_framework = MetricFramework(
    feature_name="FNOL Claims Chatbot",
    metrics=[
        # ── Strategic ──
        GenAIMetric(
            name="cost_per_fnol_claim",
            metric_type=MetricType.STRATEGIC,
            description="Fully loaded cost per FNOL claim including staff time",
            target="Reduce by 18% within 12 months of launch",
            measurement_method="Finance ledger: AI infra cost + remaining human handling time × blended rate",
            cadence="Monthly",
            owner="Finance / Programme Sponsor",
        ),

        # ── Outcome ──
        GenAIMetric(
            name="time_to_first_response",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTCOME,
            description="Wall-clock time from claim submission to claimant receiving first substantive response",
            target="≤ 4 min (baseline: 18 min)",
            measurement_method="Timestamp delta in CRM: submission → first staff or AI response logged",
            cadence="Weekly rolling average",
            owner="Claims Operations",
            leads_to="cost_per_fnol_claim",
        ),
        GenAIMetric(
            name="claimant_satisfaction_fnol",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTCOME,
            description="Post-FNOL survey score on the AI interaction",
            target="≥ 4.2 / 5.0",
            measurement_method="In-session survey (1-question NPS variant), 30% response rate sample",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="Customer Experience",
            leads_to="cost_per_fnol_claim",
        ),
        GenAIMetric(
            name="human_rework_rate",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTCOME,
            description="Fraction of AI-handled FNOLs requiring human correction within 48 hours",
            target="≤ 8% (baseline: 22%)",
            measurement_method="CRM flag: 'AI outcome amended' within 48 h of chatbot session close",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="Claims Quality",
            leads_to="cost_per_fnol_claim",
        ),

        # ── Output ──
        GenAIMetric(
            name="intent_classification_accuracy",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTPUT,
            description="Fraction of user messages correctly routed to claim type",
            target="≥ 94%",
            measurement_method="Weekly sample (n=200) reviewed by QA analysts",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="AI Quality Team",
            leads_to="human_rework_rate",
        ),
        GenAIMetric(
            name="escalation_rate",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTPUT,
            description="Fraction of sessions handed off to human agent",
            target="≤ 15%",
            measurement_method="Session log: escalation event count / total sessions",
            cadence="Daily",
            owner="AI Quality Team",
            leads_to="time_to_first_response",
        ),
        GenAIMetric(
            name="hallucination_rate",
            metric_type=MetricType.OUTPUT,
            description="Fraction of factual claims not supported by cited source",
            target="≤ 2%",
            measurement_method="LLM-judge on weekly 100-interaction sample",
            cadence="Weekly",
            owner="AI Quality Team",
            leads_to="human_rework_rate",
        ),
    ],
)

errors = fnol_framework.validate()
print(errors)   # [] — all outputs linked, outcomes present

outcome_metrics = fnol_framework.by_type(MetricType.OUTCOME)
print([m.name for m in outcome_metrics])
# ['time_to_first_response', 'claimant_satisfaction_fnol', 'human_rework_rate']
The following output metrics are common in first-draft dashboards and consistently fail to provide insight into business value. Each has a direct outcome-linked replacement.

# Anti-pattern: vanity metric dashboard

# These are common first-draft "success metrics" for GenAI features.
# None of them tell you whether the product is delivering business value.

VANITY_METRICS = {
    "total_sessions":              "Counts usage; doesn't distinguish helpful from harmful interactions",
    "tokens_generated_per_day":    "Measures AI activity; goes up whether quality improves or degrades",
    "average_session_length":      "Longer isn't better — it may mean users can't find what they need",
    "model_accuracy_on_test_set":  "Benchmark accuracy doesn't transfer to production query distribution",
    "number_of_features_shipped":  "Output metric for the team, not the product",
    "five_star_ratings_count":     "Denominator is missing; 100 five-stars out of 10,000 is 1%",
}

# Replace each with an outcome-linked metric:
OUTCOME_REPLACEMENTS = {
    "total_sessions":           "task_completion_rate (sessions reaching stated goal)",
    "tokens_generated_per_day": "cost_per_successful_interaction",
    "average_session_length":   "time_to_task_completion (shorter is usually better)",
    "model_accuracy_on_test_set": "human_rework_rate (did the AI answer hold up in production?)",
    "number_of_features_shipped": "features_adopted_by_target_users_at_30_days",
    "five_star_ratings_count":  "claimant_satisfaction_score (mean, with sample size)",
}

The most insidious vanity metric is test-set accuracy. A model that achieves 96% accuracy on the development benchmark and 91% on production traffic has a 5-point gap that the test-set metric never reveals. Always validate output metrics on a production traffic sample, not a fixed evaluation set.
Verifying That Output Metrics Predict Outcomes

The causal chain from output to outcome is a hypothesis, not a fact. Verify it with correlation analysis over at least 10 weeks of production data before treating an output metric as a reliable leading indicator.

# Sensitivity analysis: is the output metric actually predictive?

import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

# Weekly data: output metric values and outcome metric values
escalation_rates = [0.18, 0.14, 0.12, 0.16, 0.10, 0.13, 0.11, 0.15, 0.09, 0.12]
rework_rates     = [0.24, 0.19, 0.16, 0.21, 0.14, 0.17, 0.15, 0.20, 0.12, 0.16]

r, p_value = stats.pearsonr(escalation_rates, rework_rates)
print(f"Pearson r = {r:.2f}, p = {p_value:.4f}")
# Pearson r = 0.98, p = 0.0000  → strong predictive relationship confirmed

# If r < 0.6 or p > 0.05, the output metric is not a reliable leading indicator
# and should be replaced with one that correlates with the outcome

When correlation is weak (r < 0.6) or statistically insignificant (p > 0.05), the output metric is not predicting the outcome you care about. Either the causal mechanism is wrong, or a confounding variable is driving the outcome. Replace the metric rather than reporting a number that misleads.

Stakeholders typically describe GenAI success in output terms ('the AI should be accurate', 'it should be fast') because those are the properties they can imagine. Use counterfactual and consequence questions to surface the underlying outcome.
'If it gives a wrong answer on 1 in 20 claims, what happens? Who fixes it, and how long does that take?'
'How long does it currently take a claimant to get a first response? What would a good result look like in 6 months?'
Time to first response ≤ 4 min (from 18 min)
'If you saw the satisfaction score, what number would make you confident this is working? What would make you shut it down?'
'What is the current fully-loaded cost per claim? What percentage reduction would justify the investment within 12 months?'
'What happens when the AI can't handle a query? Is the fallback to a human? How many of those are acceptable?'
Escalation rate ≤ 15% (leading); human headcount held flat (outcome)
Anti-Pattern: Reporting Only Output Metrics to Sponsors

The single most damaging reporting mistake is presenting only AI-layer metrics in stakeholder reviews. When a sponsor sees a dashboard of latency, hallucination rate, and session counts without any connection to business outcomes, they cannot assess ROI. After two or three reviews with no business data, confidence in the programme collapses — regardless of technical quality.

The engineering team reported that the chatbot had a 96% intent classification accuracy and 800 ms P95 latency. The business sponsor asked how many fewer claims were being reworked. Nobody knew. The programme was paused for an impact assessment six months after launch.
The fix is structural: the outcome metric and its measurement method must be agreed and baselined before the feature launches, not retrofitted when sponsors ask for it.

Outcome and output metrics map naturally to the OKR (Objectives and Key Results) structure. The objective is the strategic outcome; key results are the outcome metrics; initiatives are the work that moves output metrics that are expected to drive the key results.
Reduce claims handling cost by 18% within FY26
Reduce time to first claimant response from 18 min to ≤ 4 min
Achieve claimant satisfaction score ≥ 4.2 on FNOL interaction
Reduce human rework rate from 22% to ≤ 8%
Reduce escalation rate to ≤ 15% (expected to drive KR1 and KR3)

Output metrics do not belong in the OKR itself — they are the team's internal leading indicators for whether an initiative is on track to move a key result. Mixing them in dilutes the OKR with operational detail and obscures whether the business is winning.

# Recommended metric review cadence for GenAI features

REVIEW_SCHEDULE = {
    "daily": [
        "Escalation rate to human agent",
        "Error / exception rate in AI pipeline",
        "P95 latency (automated alert only)",
    ],
    "weekly": [
        "Hallucination rate (LLM-judge on sample)",
        "Claimant satisfaction score (rolling)",
        "Human rework rate",
        "Cost per interaction",
        "Intent classification accuracy (QA sample)",
    ],
    "monthly": [
        "Time to first response (ops trend)",
        "Cost per FNOL claim (finance ledger)",
        "Fairness / disparity audit",
        "Metric–outcome correlation review",
    ],
    "quarterly": [
        "Strategic outcome progress (sponsor review)",
        "OKR scoring and reset",
        "Metric framework validity check — prune stale metrics, add new ones",
    ],
}

The correlation between output and outcome metrics should itself be reviewed quarterly. Output metrics that no longer predict outcomes should be retired; new output metrics that better explain outcome variance should be added. A static metric framework is a decaying asset.

Every outcome metric requires a pre-launch baseline. Without it, the target is arbitrary and the post-launch measurement is unanchored. For a new GenAI feature replacing a human process, the baseline is the current process's performance on the same metric.
Finance ledger: staff time × rate + overhead

If no baseline data exists, a two-to-four week measurement sprint against the current process must be run before development begins. Launching without a baseline makes impact measurement impossible and retrospective justification impossible.
Common Mistakes in GenAI Metric Design
Cannot assess business value; sponsor confidence collapses
Define at least one outcome metric per strategic objective before development starts
Post-launch data has no comparison point
Run baseline measurement sprint (2–4 weeks) before development
Arbitrary targets are usually wrong; hitting them proves nothing
Set targets from baseline × improvement ambition
Treating test-set accuracy as a production metric
Distribution shift means production accuracy differs from benchmark
Measure accuracy on production-sampled interactions weekly
Output metric dashboard in sponsor reviews
Sponsors cannot assess ROI; disconnect causes loss of confidence
Lead sponsor reviews with outcome metrics; use output metrics to explain
Output metrics stop predicting outcomes as system evolves
Quarterly review of output→outcome correlation; retire and replace as needed
Worked Example: Designing a Metric Framework from Scratch
The following process takes a new GenAI feature from a business objective statement to a validated, three-layer metric framework. The example is a document summarisation tool for a legal team.
Start with the strategic outcome: 'Reduce time lawyers spend on first-read contract review by 40% within 9 months.'

Identify what must change in user behaviour to achieve it — these become outcome metrics: (a) mean time to complete first-read review, (b) fraction of contracts requiring no re-read after AI summary, (c) lawyer satisfaction score on AI summary quality.

Baseline each outcome metric before building anything: pull calendar and document system logs to measure current first-read review times; survey lawyers on satisfaction with the current (manual) process.

For each outcome metric, identify the AI system behaviour most likely to predict it — these become output metrics: (a) summary completeness score (were all material clauses captured?), (b) summary accuracy score (were no clauses misrepresented?), (c) time to generate summary.
Assign an owner and a measurement method to every metric. Output metrics without owners are never measured in production.
After 8 weeks of production data, run a correlation analysis between each output metric and its linked outcome metric. Replace any output metric with r < 0.6.
One sentence: what changes in the business
2–4 measurable behaviour changes, each with a target
Current-state measurements for each outcome metric
1–3 AI behaviour metrics per outcome metric
Named individual for each metric's measurement cadence
r ≥ 0.6 for each output → outcome pair
The entire process takes 2–3 weeks when run before development starts and saves months of post-launch debate about whether the product is working.

GenAI output metrics are sensitive to model version changes, prompt updates, and retrieval index refreshes. Every such change should trigger a metric sensitivity check: run the full output metric suite against a held-out interaction sample before and after the change and compare the distributions.
Hallucination rate, intent accuracy, tone consistency
Run before cut-over; parallel traffic for 1 week
Scope violation rate, refusal rate, citation coverage
Retrieval precision, citation accuracy, completeness score
Nightly diff on held-out query set
Response diversity, hallucination rate, length distribution
Refusal false-positive rate, scope violation rate

The most dangerous model change is a silent one: a provider silently rolls out a new model version under the same API endpoint. Establish a canary query set that runs hourly and alerts on output metric drift greater than 1 standard deviation from the 30-day rolling baseline.

# Canary monitoring: detect silent model version changes
import statistics

def detect_metric_drift(
    current_week: list[float],
    baseline_30d: list[float],
    metric_name: str,
    threshold_sigma: float = 1.0,
) -> bool:
    """
    Returns True if current week's metric mean deviates > threshold_sigma
    standard deviations from the 30-day baseline.
    """
    baseline_mean = statistics.mean(baseline_30d)
    baseline_std  = statistics.stdev(baseline_30d)
    current_mean  = statistics.mean(current_week)

    if baseline_std == 0:
        return False  # no variance in baseline — skip check

    sigma_deviation = abs(current_mean - baseline_mean) / baseline_std
    if sigma_deviation > threshold_sigma:
        print(
            f"ALERT: {metric_name} drifted {sigma_deviation:.1f}σ "
            f"(baseline {baseline_mean:.3f}, current {current_mean:.3f})"
        )
        return True
    return False

# Example: hallucination rate drift detection
baseline = [0.018, 0.021, 0.019, 0.022, 0.017, 0.020, 0.019, 0.021]  # 30d daily values
current  = [0.031, 0.034, 0.029, 0.033]                                # this week

drifted = detect_metric_drift(current, baseline, "hallucination_rate")
# ALERT: hallucination_rate drifted 2.3σ (baseline 0.020, current 0.032)

Output metrics measure what the AI does; outcome metrics measure what changes as a result. Enterprise GenAI features require both, linked in a causal chain and verified by correlation analysis. Strategic outcomes (the business goal) are explained by outcome metrics (operational changes in user behaviour), which are predicted by output metrics (AI system performance). Reporting only output metrics to sponsors severs the connection to value and erodes programme confidence. Every outcome metric needs a pre-launch baseline, a numeric target, an owner, and a defined measurement method. The output-to-outcome causal chain is a hypothesis that must be validated with production data — and revised when correlation does not hold.
